author_background: Former marine biologist and university professor specializing in coral reef ecosystems. Married to Lena for 12 years, father to 7-year-old Maya. Was researching thermal resilience in Pacific corals when the outbreak began. Dreamed of establishing marine protected areas and taking his daughter diving on the Great Barrier Reef.
location: Abandoned coastal research station, Oregon - perched on crumbling cliffs overlooking a perpetually gray ocean. The building groans with every gust of wind, salt crusting every surface. Faded marine charts still cling to the walls, their corners curling like dead leaves.
entry: The rain hasn't stopped for three days. It drums against the corrugated roof in a rhythm that's become the soundtrack to my madness. I found Maya's drawing today - tucked between pages of my old research notes. She'd drawn us as mermaids, swimming through colorful coral gardens. Her little stick-figure version of me held a magnifying glass instead of a trident. I cried for the first time in months. Not the silent tears that sometimes escape, but great, heaving sobs that left me raw. Lena would have known what to say. She always did. Yesterday, I had to make a choice that's been eating at me. A family - mother, father, young boy - came to the station begging for shelter. They'd been traveling for weeks. The boy had that glassy-eyed look, the early stages of infection. I turned them away. I listened to their pleading until their voices faded into the storm. What kind of man have I become? The kind that survives, I suppose. But at what cost? Tonight, I'm sitting with the ghost of who I was, surrounded by the ruins of everything I loved. The waves keep crashing below, indifferent to our suffering. Maybe that's the hardest lesson - the world doesn't care about our morality plays. It just... continues.
condition: Physically: Malnourished (down 15kg), chronic cough from damp conditions, left hand infected from rusty metal cut. Mentally: Depressive episodes alternating with hyper-vigilance, experiencing vivid dreams of pre-outbreak life, struggling with guilt over survival decisions.
discoveries: The infection spreads faster in cold, wet conditions - symptoms appear within hours instead of days. Rainwater collected in certain plastic containers develops a toxic film after 48 hours. The infected show aversion to strong saltwater concentrations.
warnings: Never trust clear water - always boil twice. The infected can mimic human crying sounds to lure survivors. Coastal areas may seem safer but attract desperate people. Keep moving, even when every bone screams to stay put.
last_meal: 12 hours ago - canned sardines (found in station pantry) mixed with boiled dandelion greens, washed down with carefully filtered rainwater.
companions: Alone. Lost Lena on Day 43 to infection after she took a risk to get medicine for Maya. Maya... didn't make it through the first month. The fever took her in her sleep. Sometimes I still wake up thinking I hear her singing.
